summary: Agricultural calendar from Nazca, held at the Museum fur Volkerkunde Berlin. 285 cords, 8 STRING (3%), 100% hits. Architecture 1-PAIRE, depth L3, 21 clusters, 4 colors (B dominant at 41%). Date: 1460/09 CE Mode A — early Inca administration at Nazca. Vocabulary: kaqa x2 (wall-AY, Aymara = coastal zone confirmed), kaki x1 (Pleiades/dried corn = planting signal), taki x1 (sowing chant), wama x1 (falcon/hawk = seasonal marker), qaqa x1 (rock = field landmark), chaqa x1 (bridge = irrigation canal crossing). Agricultural register with astronomical timing elements.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  285 cords, depth L3 — deep hierarchy with subsidiaries.
  Architecture: 1-PAIRE, 21 clusters.
  Colors: B(117 = 41%), W(70 = 25%), BB(59 = 21%), G(39 = 14%) — 4 colors.
  8 STRING (3%), 100% hit rate. 250 INT, 27 EMPTY.
  Date: 1460/09 CE Mode A — early Inca period at Nazca.
  Provenance: NAZCA — same as UR113 (Valhalla) and UR198 (census).
  Museum: Museum fur Volkerkunde, Berlin.
  OKR: Diagram of marker attachment (Ascher & Ascher 1987:891).

COLOR-AS-ROLE
  B (brown, 117 cords = 41%): Dominant — the primary accounting category. Possibly the main crop (maize?).
  W (white, 70 cords = 25%): Second category — a secondary crop or season.
  BB (dark blue-brown, 59 cords = 21%): Third category.
  G (green, 39 cords = 14%): Fourth category — possibly associated with irrigation or a specific crop.
  Four colors may encode four crops or four seasons across twenty-one clusters.

KEY VOCABULARY
  kaqa x2 = wall (Aymara) — terrace boundaries. Aymara in Nazca = coastal zone confirmed.
  kaki x1 = Pleiades/dried corn — dual meaning: the star AND the crop it signals.
  taki x1 = sowing chant/Saturn — the ritual song that accompanies planting.
  wama x1 = falcon/hawk — in agricultural context, a seasonal marker (the hawk's appearance signals season change).
  piki x1 = flea — pest? or metaphorical?
  chaqa x1 = bridge — crosses an irrigation canal.
  qaqa x1 = rock — field landmark.

STRUCTURE
  285 cords in 21 clusters = ~13.6 cords per cluster. The L3 depth (3 levels of subsidiaries) suggests hierarchical accounting: cluster > group > sub-entry.
  250 INT cords = 88% numerical — this is primarily a quantitative register.
  27 EMPTY cords = cluster separators and phase breaks.
  8 STRING cords (3%) carry the vocabulary that identifies the document type.

CROSS-CORPUS — NAZCA TRIO
  UR1136 joins UR113 (Valhalla) and UR198 (census) as the third major khipu from Nazca. While UR113 and UR198 are administrative/census documents, UR1136 is agricultural — it tracks what the land produces, not who lives on it.

NARRATIVE
  The hawk circles above the fields of Nazca. Wama — the falcon, the seasonal marker — appears in a register of 285 cords dated to 1460, eight years after the Inca first extended their administration to the southern coast. The walls (kaqa, Aymara, twice) bound the terraces where corn (kaki) grows — the same word that names the Pleiades, because the Pleiades tell when to plant. The sowing chant (taki) sets the rhythm. A rock (qaqa) marks a field landmark. A bridge (chaqa) crosses an irrigation canal. Four colors — brown, white, dark blue-brown, green — may encode four crops or four seasons across twenty-one clusters.

  The hawk watches, the Pleiades rise, and the scribe counts what the land produces in the year 1460 on the Nazca plain. Two hundred and fifty numerical cords organized in twenty-one groups with three levels of hierarchy — this is not a note but a ledger, an annual accounting of harvests and stores, timed to the stars and marked by the falcon's flight. The Aymara word for wall (kaqa) confirms the coastal linguistic zone, the same bilingual territory where Inca administrators recorded in two languages. At Berlin's ethnological museum, this khipu preserves the harvest calendar of a people who watched the sky to know when to plant.
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Agricultural calendar analysis, Nazca provenance
Dating paper: Sivan 2026, 'Dating the Inca Archive' — UR1136 Mode A date 1460/09 CE
Ascher & Ascher 1987:891 — Diagram of marker attachment (OKR notes)
Urton, G. (2003). Signs of the Inka Khipu — structure and classification
